1: וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ וְאֶת-בָּנָיו — "And God blessed Noah and his sons." The blessing formula repeats 1:28 exactly: pĕrû ûrĕbû ûmilĕʾû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ (be fruitful, multiply, fill the earth). The post-flood world begins with the same creation-blessing as the first world — Noah and his family are the second Adam, receiving the same mandate to multiply and fill. The repetition of the original blessing emphasizes continuity across the flood's discontinuity: creation's mandate stands.
2: וּמוֹרַאֲכֶם וְחִתְּכֶם (ûmôrăʾăḵem wĕḥittĕḵem) — "your fear and your dread." The noun pair môrāʾ (fear, terror) and ḥittāh (dread, terror) describes the changed relationship between humanity and the animal kingdom. In 1:28 and Genesis 2:19-20 (the original creation mandate and the naming of animals), there is no mention of animal fear — the relationship was harmonious dominion. Now, after the Fall and Flood, the relationship is maintained by fear rather than natural co-operation. This is the realistic acknowledgment that the post-Fall world operates on power rather than harmony.
3: כָּל-רֶמֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר הוּא-חַי לָכֶם יִהְיֶה לְאָכְלָה — "every moving thing that lives shall be food for you." The original food grant (Genesis 1:29-30) limited humans to seed-bearing plants and fruit — meat was not permitted. Now God explicitly grants meat-eating: "just as I gave you the green plants, I give you everything." This is a new permission, not a clarification of an original permission. The grant is universal within the animal kingdom subject only to the blood restriction of Genesis 9:4.
4: אַךְ-בָּשָׂר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ — "but flesh with its nepeš — its blood — you shall not eat." The contrastive particle ʾaḵ (but, however, only) marks the single exception to the universal food grant. The syntax is compressed: flesh + bĕnaphšô (in its life/soul) = dāmô (its blood). The equation nepeš = dām (life = blood) is spelled out explicitly in Leviticus 17:11 ("the life of the flesh is in the blood") and Genesis 17:14 ("the life of every creature is its blood"). The blood prohibition grounds the Levitical blood-atonement system (Leviticus 17:11: "I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your lives; it is the blood that makes atonement by the life") in the Noahic covenant — the blood belongs to God because life belongs to God.
5: וְאַךְ אֶת-דִּמְכֶם לְנַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם אֶדְרֹשׁ — "and surely for your blood I will require a reckoning, for your lives." Dāraš (to seek out, require, demand) is the legal-investigative term — God as the investigator of bloodshed. The accountability is from two sources: (1) from every animal (miḵkol-ḥayyāh) that kills a human — an extension of human accountability into the animal kingdom (cf. Exodus 21:28-29, the goring ox); (2) from every human who kills another human. The structure expands the reach of divine justice beyond human-to-human violence.
6: שֹׁפֵךְ דַּם הָאָדָם בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ — "whoever sheds the blood of a human (hāʾādām), by a human (bāʾādām) shall his blood be shed." The chiastic structure in Hebrew is formally perfect: šōpēḵ dām hāʾādām / bāʾādām / dāmô yiššāpēḵ. The three elements (blood + the human + blood) frame the middle term (by a human) — the punishment mirrors the crime in both substance (blood for blood) and agent (human for human). כִּי בְּצֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה אֶת-הָאָדָם — "for in the image of God he made humankind." The kî (because, for) grounds the capital sanction in the image-of-God status: to murder a human is to assault God's image-representative on earth. Crucially, the image-of-God status is affirmed as still operative after the Fall and after the Flood — it was not destroyed by sin, only damaged or distorted.
7: The fruitfulness mandate of Genesis 9:1 is repeated verbatim — the divine speech brackets the blood laws (Genesis 9:2-6) with the creation blessing (Genesis 9:1) and its restatement (Genesis 9:7). The structure: blessing → permitted food (Genesis 9:3) → forbidden food/blood (Genesis 9:4) → blood accountability (Genesis 9:5-6) → blessing repeated. The repeating blessing frames the new laws as operating within the context of divine generosity and life-promotion, not mere restriction.
8: The divine speech of covenant-establishment begins with the double address: "God said to Noah and to his sons with him" — the covenant is addressed to both the immediate recipients (Noah) and the next generation (his sons). The covenant is dynastic and generational from its first formulation.
9: אֲנִי הִנְנִי מֵקִים אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אִתְּכֶם — "I, behold, I am establishing my covenant with you." The emphatic independent pronoun ʾănî (I) + the demonstrative particle hinnĕnî (behold me/I am here) + Hiphil participle mĕqîm (establishing, making to stand) — the construction stresses both the divine person ("I myself") and the ongoing act (participle = continuous action). Bĕrît (covenant): this is the first time God explicitly calls his commitment a bĕrît; earlier commitments were present but the formal covenant terminology now appears. The covenant is unilateral in its establishment — God alone speaks and commits; Noah makes no oath.
10: וְאֶת כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה — "and with every living creature (nepeš) that is with you." The Noahic covenant is unique among biblical covenants in being explicitly covenanted with non-human creatures — birds, livestock, and wild animals are all named as covenant parties (Genesis 9:10, 12, 15, 16, 17). This is a cosmic covenant, not merely a human one; God's commitment is to all animate life bearing the nepeš. The Noahic covenant encompasses the entire creational order.
11: לֹא-יִכָּרֵת כָּל-בָּשָׂר עוֹד מִמֵּי הַמַּבּוּל — "never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of a flood." The Niphal of kārat (to cut off, cut down — used for cutting covenants [kārat bĕrît, to cut a covenant] and for cutting off violators from the community) — ironically, the word for "cut off in judgment" is here used for the divine pledge that the ultimate judgment will never recur: never again shall flesh be "cut off" by flood. The double negative (lōʾ... ʿôd) emphasizes permanence: no more, not again.
12: זֹאת אוֹת-הַבְּרִית (zōʾt ʾôt-habbĕrît) — "this is the sign of the covenant." ʾÔt (sign, mark) — the same word used for the Sabbath as a sign of the covenant (Exodus 31:13, 17), circumcision as a sign (Genesis 17:11), and the signs given through Moses (Exodus 4:8-9; Genesis 10:1-2). Every major covenant has a sign; the Noahic sign is universal and cosmic — visible to all people, not requiring circumcision or Sabbath-keeping to observe.
13: אֶת-קַשְׁתִּי נָתַתִּי בֶּעָנָן (ʾet-qašti nātattî beʿānān) — "I have set my bow in the clouds." Qešet means a bow — specifically, a warrior's bow used for shooting arrows. The same word is used of Nimrod's hunting prowess (Genesis 10:9) and of the divine warrior's bow in Psalms 7:12; Habakkuk 3:9-11. By "setting the bow in the clouds," God hangs up his weapon of war — the bow is the ANE military symbol of divine judgment; a god who lays down the bow signals the cessation of war. God hangs his weapon in the sky as a visible reminder that the war of judgment is over. The rainbow is God's war-bow, decommissioned and displayed in the sky as a memorial of covenant peace.
14: The conditional clause structure — "when I bring clouds over the earth and the bow appears in the clouds" — makes the rainbow a periodic, repeated reminder rather than a one-time sign. The clouds (ʿānān) were associated with divine presence and theophany (Exodus 13:21-22; Genesis 19:16-19; 1 Kings 8:10-11; Ezekiel 1:4) — God's presence in the storm-cloud that might have brought flood is now the occasion for the bow's appearance, which interposes the covenant-reminder between the threatening cloud and the earth below.
15: וְזָכַרְתִּי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי (wĕzāḵartî ʾet-bĕrîtî) — "and I will remember my covenant." Zākar again in the covenant-faithfulness sense (cf. Genesis 8:1). God does not forget the covenant, but the remembering-language places the covenant on the side of divine faithfulness rather than human performance — the rainbow reminds God (from the human narrative perspective) and humanity simultaneously. The waters will never again become a flood to destroy all flesh — kol-bāśār (all flesh): the comprehensive formula of Genesis 9:11, 15, 16, 17 reinforces totality.
16: בְּרִית עוֹלָם (bĕrît ʿôlām) — "an everlasting covenant." ʿÔlām (eternity, perpetuity, from of old and into the far future) qualifies the covenant as without temporal limit. The same phrase is used for the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 17:7, 13, 19), the Mosaic covenant in its application (Exodus 31:16; Leviticus 24:8), and the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 23:5; Psalms 89:28). The Noahic covenant is the most universally inclusive of all biblical covenants — it encompasses all living creatures and all generations.
17: God reiterates the sign-identification for clarity: "This is the sign of the covenant I have established between me and all flesh that is on the earth." The verse closes the covenant-establishment speech with a summary statement, a standard narrative technique (the "colophon" that closes a section by restating its content). The triple use of bĕrît (covenant) in this verse (in the Hebrew: haqqeṣet... bĕrît... bêynî ûbêyn kol-bāśār) reinforces the covenant's centrality to this entire section.
18: חָם הוּא אֲבִי כְנָעַן (ḥām hûʾ ʾăbî kĕnaʿan) — "Ham was the father of Canaan." The parenthetical identification of Ham as Canaan's father anticipates the curse of Genesis 9:25 — the reader is being prepared by noting the connection between Ham and Canaan before the incident that will curse Canaan. The three sons of Noah (Shem, Ham, Japheth) are the ancestors of the nations in Genesis 10, giving the Ham/Canaan/curse narrative its geopolitical implications for Israel's later relationship with Canaan.
19: מֵאֵלֶּה נָפְצָה כָל-הָאָרֶץ (mēʾēlleh nāpĕṣāh kol-hāʾāreṣ) — "from these the whole earth was scattered/spread out." Nāpaṣ (to scatter, spread) — the same verb as in the Babel narrative (Genesis 11:4, 8, 9), where God scatters the nations. The "spreading out" here is the positive peopling of the earth fulfilling the creation mandate; the scattering of Babel will be its divinely-imposed intensification. The bridge between the Noahic genealogical promise and the Babel dispersion runs through this verse.
20: וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה (wayyāḥel nōaḥ ʾîš hāʾăḏāmāh) — "Noah, a man of the soil, began." Hēḥēl is the Hiphil of ḥālal — "began" or "profaned/desecrated." The same form can mean either "began" or "made common/profane" (cf. Genesis 4:26: hûḥal, "at that time people began to call on the name of the LORD"). The ambiguity may be intentional: Noah began as a man of the ground, or Noah, a man of the ground, profaned himself. ʾÎš hāʾăḏāmāh (man of the soil) is the same construction as ʾādām min-hāʾăḏāmāh (Adam from the ground, Genesis 2:7; Genesis 3:19) — Noah is the new Adam, returning to work the ground.
21: וַיִּשְׁתְּ מִן-הַיַּיִן וַיִּשְׁכָּר — "and he drank from the wine and became drunk." The verb šākar (to become drunk) is used neutrally here — no explicit moral judgment on the drunkenness itself. וַיִּתְגַּל (wayyitgal) — "and he uncovered himself" — Hithpael of gālāh (to uncover, reveal). The reflexive (Hithpael) makes this self-exposure: Noah uncovered himself within his tent. The nakedness (ʿerwāh implied) connects back to 2:25 and Genesis 3:7 — nakedness in the biblical world is associated with vulnerability, shame, and violation.
22: וַיַּרְא חָם אֲבִי כְנָעַן אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיו — "Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father." Rāʾāh ʿerwāh (to see nakedness) is the legal phrase in Leviticus 20:17-19 for forbidden sexual viewing/violation — "if a man takes his sister... and sees her nakedness (rāʾāh ʾet-ʿerwātāh)... it is a disgrace." The phrase in Leviticus consistently connotes sexual transgression, not accidental observation. The mildest interpretation (Ham accidentally saw, then gossiped about it to his brothers) does not fully account for the severity of the curse on Canaan or the use of this specific technical phrase. At minimum, Ham violated his father's dignity by observing and reporting rather than covering; at maximum, the phrase implies a more serious violation. The text leaves the specific act deliberately ambiguous.
23: וַיִּקַּח שֵׁם וָיֶפֶת אֶת-הַשִּׂמְלָה — "Shem and Japheth took a garment." The contrast is explicit: Ham saw and told; Shem and Japheth took a garment and covered. עַל-שְׁכֶם שְׁנֵיהֶם (ʿal-šĕkem šĕnêhem) — "on the shoulders of both of them" — both carry the garment together, walking backward (wayyĕlĕḵû ʾăḥōranît) so as not to see their father's nakedness. The backward-walking detail is not merely practical but honor-protective: they refuse even accidental sight. Their act of covering reverses Ham's act of exposing — shame covered vs. shame revealed.
24: מַה-עָּשָׂה לוֹ בְּנוֹ הַקָּטָן — "what his youngest son had done to him." The narrative describes Ham as the "youngest son" — but in the standard genealogical list, Ham is the middle son (Shem, Ham, Japheth in Genesis 5:32; Genesis 6:10; Genesis 7:13; Genesis 9:18; Genesis 10:1). Either "youngest" (qāṭōn) refers to relative status or honor-position in this context rather than birth order, or there is a tradition that Ham was in fact the youngest. Noah's "finding out what his son had done" implies that what Ham did was a discovered act, not merely observed — the detail that Shem and Japheth specifically did not see their father (Genesis 9:23) implies that what there was to see was something shameful that Ham saw (and possibly did).
25: אָרוּר כְּנָעַן (ʾārûr kĕnaʿan) — "cursed be Canaan." The curse falls on Canaan, Ham's son, rather than on Ham directly. The explanation commonly offered: cursing the son rather than the father may reflect the ancient Near Eastern legal-rhetorical convention in which the consequences of a father's sin fall on his lineage (cf. Exodus 20:5), or may reflect that Ham's sin was done through Canaan (the text is silent on this). Theologically significant: the curse does not fall on Shem or Japheth's descendants, shaping the later Israelite understanding of Canaan's relationship to Israel. ʿEbed ʿăbādîm — "servant of servants" — the superlative construction (like "holy of holies" = most holy, "king of kings" = supreme king) means "the lowest possible servant," the ultimate subordinate.
26: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי שֵׁם — "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem." The blessing of Shem is structured as a blessing of God who is Shem's God — the covenantal name YHWH appears here, connecting Shem's line with the covenant name. The Messianic line running through Shem (to Abraham, to Israel, to Christ) makes this blessing the covenantal promise: the LORD-covenanted God is the God of Shem's descendants. Canaan is servant to Shem — implying Israel's eventual conquest of Canaan under Joshua.
27: יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת (yapet ʾĕlōhîm lĕyepet) — "May God enlarge Japheth." The wordplay is explicit: yāpat (enlarge, make wide) on Japheth (yepet). The blessing of Japheth is territorial expansion (pāṭāh = to be wide, open). וְיִשְׁכֹּן בְּאָהֳלֵי-שֵׁם — "and let him dwell in the tents of Shem." The subject of "dwell" is ambiguous: either Japheth dwells in Shem's tents (a sign of blessing — sharing the covenant space), or God dwells in Shem's tents (God's presence among Shem's descendants). The ambiguity is interpretively productive: both readings are theologically rich.
28: The genealogical framing verse closes the Noahic narrative. וַיְחִי-נֹחַ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל — "Noah lived after the flood." The wayyiqtol marks the narrative continuation into the genealogical register. Noah lives 350 more years after the flood — his total of 950 years (Genesis 9:29) makes him the second-longest-lived of the antediluvian patriarchs (only Methuselah at 969 years exceeds him). The post-flood lifespans begin declining in Genesis 11, marking the transition from the antediluvian world-era to the patriarchal era.
29: וַיָּמֹת (wayyāmōt) — "and he died." The genealogical death formula closes the Noahic narrative. The structure of Genesis 5 (so-and-so lived X years, fathered Y, lived Z more years, and he died) is here abbreviated to the death notice only. Noah stands at the pivot of the flood narrative: the last of the antediluvian patriarchs, the first man of the post-flood world, the one through whom all humanity descends after the flood. His death closes the flood narrative and opens the space for the Table of Nations (Genesis 10) and Babel (Genesis 11).
